coredata.dc:title: Terbium-triggered aggregation-induced emission of bimetallic nanoclusters for anticancer drugs sensing via the inner filter effect
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mu, Jin
Zhang, Huifeng
Huang, Zhenzhen
Jia, Qiong
coredata.dc:description: The development of accurate and sensitive detection methods of anticancer drugs is of significant importance because they play vital roles in biological systems. In recent years, bimetallic nanoclusters (BMNCs) incorporating the advantages of two metals have gained more and more attention, and can be widely applied in sensing applications. In this work, for the first time, we designed a sensing platform based on terbium ion (Tb3+) triggered aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of BMNCs. Tb3+ hybrid glutathione (GS) protected Ag/Cu nanoclusters (Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs) were facilely fabricated according to the complexation reaction between Tb3+ and the carboxyl group of GS. Due to the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs decreased significantly in the presence of anticancer drugs with 6-thioguanine and methotrexate as representatives. In addition, the sensing platform was applied to monitor 6-thioguanine and methotrexate in real serum samples, indicating that it has great potential in anticancer drugs related applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence
Aggregation-induced emission
Bimetallic nanoclusters
Anticancer drugs
Inner filter effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing mechanism of TG and MTX
Optimization for TG and MTX sensing
Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs Based assay for TG and MTX sensing
Detection of TG and MTX in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis
Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission, China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work was supported by Changchun Science and Technology Bureau (CC202110361510018101) and State Key Laboratory of Inorganic Synthesis, Preparative Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, China (2022-21).
The work was supported by Changchun Science and Technology Bureau, China (21ZGN35) and Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission, China (2022C010).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) FT-IR spectra of GS, GS-CuNCs, GS-AgCuNCs, and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. (B) Zeta potential of GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs.
XPS spectra of (A) GS-AgCuNCs and (B) Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. Ag 3d, Cu 2p, C 1 s, and O 1 s XPS spectra of (C, E, G, I) GS-AgCuNCs and (D, F, H, J) Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs.

TEM images of (A) GS-AgCuNCs and (B) Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. (C) EDX elemental mapping of C, N, O, S, Cu, Ag, Tb elements performed on the area of the sample shown in (B). DLS size distribution of (D) GS-AgCuNCs and (E) Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs.

(A) Fluorescence emission and (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of GS-CuNCs, GS-AgCuNCs, and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. Fluorescence intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs under different (C) NaCl concentrations, (D) UV irradiation, (E) pH, and (F) Storage time.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of TG/MTX and fluorescence excitation (λ em = 564 nm)/emission (λ ex = 352 nm) spectra of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. (B) Fluorescence lifetime of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs, Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs + TG, and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs + MTX.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs with the addition of TG with various concentrations. (B) Linear relationship between F/F 0 and TG concentrations (F and F 0 are fluorescence intensities of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs in the presence and absence of TG). F/F 0 of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs at 564 nm upon the addition of various interferents in the (C) absence and (D) presence of TG.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs with the addition of MTX with various concentrations. (B) Linear relationship between F/F 0 and MTX concentrations (F and F 0 are fluorescence intensities of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs in the presence and absence of MTX). F/F 0 of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs at 564 nm upon the addition of various interferents in the (C) absence and (D) presence of MTX.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs and application to the sensing of TG and MTX.

head.ce:title: Terbium-triggered aggregation-induced emission of bimetallic nanoclusters for anticancer drugs sensing via the inner filter effect

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology, Jilin Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Changchun 130033, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Agricultural Quality Standards and Testing Technology, Jilin Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Changchun 130033, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of accurate and sensitive detection methods of anticancer drugs is of significant importance because they play vital roles in biological systems. In recent years, bimetallic nanoclusters (BMNCs) incorporating the advantages of two metals have gained more and more attention, and can be widely applied in sensing applications. In this work, for the first time, we designed a sensing platform based on terbium ion (Tb3+) triggered aggregation-induced emission (AIE) of BMNCs. Tb3+ hybrid glutathione (GS) protected Ag/Cu nanoclusters (Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs) were facilely fabricated according to the complexation reaction between Tb3+ and the carboxyl group of GS. Due to the inner filter effect (IFE), the fluorescence of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs decreased significantly in the presence of anticancer drugs with 6-thioguanine and methotrexate as representatives. In addition, the sensing platform was applied to monitor 6-thioguanine and methotrexate in real serum samples, indicating that it has great potential in anticancer drugs related applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Currently, various anticancer drugs are available in the market that play a pivotal role in cancer treatment. Nevertheless, their dosage is crucial, and excessive anticancer drugs dose will lead to serious side effects. For instance, 6-thioguanine (6-TG) and methotrexate (MTX) are commonly used anticancer drugs for acute lymphoblastic leukemia [1,2], while the excessive 6-TG and MTX dose may pose some life-threatening side effects, such as bone marrow depression, gastrointestinal complication [3] and pancreatitis, hepatotoxicity, renal failure [4–7]. In clinical practice, monitoring the concentration of anticancer drugs can minimize their toxicity, thereby ensuring their effectiveness in the treatment [8]. Based on this, sensitive and selective analysis of anticancer drugs is of great necessity. At present, various techniques have been exploited for monitoring the concentration of anticancer drugs, including electrochemical method [9], mass spectrometry [10], high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], etc. Unfortunately, these methods usually require expensive equipment, complex sample preparation, or long analysis time. Thus, there is an urgent need to explore uncomplicated, fast, and sensitive methods to detect anticancer drugs.

In recent years, fluorescence-based detection platforms have attracted widespread research interest due to their merits of simple equipment, short time-consuming, and low operating cost [12]. After decades of development, fluorescent sensors designed using metal nanoclusters (MNCs) have gained particular attention because of their outstanding optical properties and low toxicity. Nevertheless, most researches are focused on the synthesis and application of monometallic NCs, such as gold nanoclusters (AuNCs), silver nanoclusters (AgNCs), and copper nanoclusters (CuNCs). Recently, bimetallic NCs (BMNCs) obtained by doping MNCs with foreign metal atoms have gained much attention based on their excellent electronic, catalytic, and optical properties [13]. Compared with monometallic NCs, BMNCs exhibit higher stability and stronger fluorescence intensity because they can integrate advantages of the two MNCs, for example, the doping of Cu atoms in Au38(2-PET)24 NCs can significantly enhance the fluorescence of the parent Au38(2-PET)24 NCs [14,15].

As another attractive method to improve the fluorescence performance of MNCs, aggregation-induced emission (AIE) has been applied for designing various highly fluorescent MNCs [16]. AIE is a type of photophysical phenomenon in which non-emissive MNCs are excited to emit bright light in their aggregated state, especially for mercapto-modified MNCs [17]. Self-assembly routes can efficiently enable AIE in MNCs, and most of reported self-assembly routes are based on electrostatic interactions in previous literatures [18]. Unfortunately, the yielded MNCs usually exhibit unregulated morphologies, which makes it difficult to adjust their fluorescent properties. By contrast, emerging self-assembly strategies based on the coordination of metal ions with MNCs ligands to generate aggregates are more advantageous [19]. For example, Qu et al. proposed a method for the detection of urea employing Al3+ triggered AIE of CuNCs [20]. Ye and co-workers introduced Al3+ to drive the CuNCs aggregation, which greatly increased the fluorescence intensity and stability of CuNCs [19]. Zhao et al. prepared Zn2+-triggered AIE of dual ligand co-stabilized AuNCs and applied them to the detection of inorganic pyrophosphatase activity [21]. In addition to commonly used metal ions such as Al3+ and Zn2+, lanthanide ions (Ln3+) have also attracted attention for their excellent coordination ability in modulating the fluorescence properties of MNCs [22]. Ln3+-induced aggregation of MNCs limits the intramolecular vibrations and rotations of the capping ligands, thereby reducing the nonradiative relaxation of the excited states. Therefore, the fluorescence properties (e.g., emission intensity, fluorescence lifetime, etc.) of the aggregates induced by Ln3+ are improved [22]. In addition, the Ln3+-induced AIE avoids the usage of organic solvents [23]. Thus, the aggregation of MNCs triggered by Ln3+ is a promising way to tune their fluorescence properties. It was found that Eu3+, Tb3+, Sm3+, La3+ and Ce3+ can cause the aggregation of MNCs [22]. As a representative, most of Tb3+-regulated AIE studies are focused on monometallic NCs, while rare attention has been paid to BMNCs and their further applications in fluorescence sensing.

Building upon the aforementioned research results, herein, we exploited a method to synthesize a fluorescence probe by Tb3+-triggered AIE of AgCuNCs (Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs) through self-assembled coordination, and explored its application in the detection of anticancer drugs, 6-TG (hereafter abbreviated as TG) and MTX. Glutathione-protected AgCuNCs (GS-AgCuNCs) were fabricated through a facile one-pot synthesis method, and then aggregate Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs were fabricated via adding Tb3+ to GS-AgCuNCs solution, by which the fluorescence enhancement was achieved. TG and MTX significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of the probe, mainly because that the absorption spectra of MTX and TG overlapped with the excitation spectrum of the probe, which in turn led to the inner filter effect (IFE). On this basis, we designed a sensitive, rapid, and simple strategy for the detection of anticancer drugs in serum samples. To our knowledge, this is the first report for the study of the AIE properties of Tb3+ triggered BMNCs and explores its application in the determination of anticancer drugs.
Details about materials, apparatus, and experimental methods are listed in Supporting Information.

Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs based on lanthanide ion-triggered AIE. The influence of Tb3+ concentration and reaction time on the fluorescence properties of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs were investigated and displayed in Fig. S1. Fig. S1 A demonstrates that the fluorescence emission of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs is closely related to the Tb3+ concentration, and the fluorescence intensity hardly changes after the concentration is higher than 0.75 mM. Therefore, 0.75 mM was chosen as the Tb3+ concentration for subsequent probe synthesis. As depicted in Fig. S1B, we chose 10 min as the optimal reaction time to prepare Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs for subsequent studies.

Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of GS, GS-CuNCs, GS-AgCuNCs, and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs are displayed in Fig. 1A. The peak at 2522 cm−1 ascribed to -SH stretching vibration in the spectrum of GS disappears in the spectra of GS-CuNCs, GS-AgCuNCs, and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs, indicating the formation of Cu-S and/or Ag-S bond at the metal surface [24]. Moreover, the main absorption peaks of GS-CuNCs and GS-AgCuNCs almost coincide with each other. After introducing Tb3+, the peak attributing to COOH symmetric stretching vibration in GS-AgCuNCs shifts from 1396 to 1388 cm−1. Such a result indicates that the coordination between COOH group and Tb3+ occurs, which was also demonstrated by zeta-potential results (Fig. 1B). As we can see, GS-AgCuNCs have a negative surface charge of −7.58 mV owing to numerous COOH groups. However, the surface charge of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs is −0.13 mV, verifying the strong coordination between COOH group and Tb3+.

X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) measurement was applied to confirm the element components of GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. Compared with GS-CuNCs (Fig. S2), GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs display a peak signal at approx. 368.3 eV, confirming the successful doping of Ag (Fig. 2 A and B). As shown in Fig. 2C and 2D, the binding energies of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 are situated at 374.2 and 368.1 eV, which illustrates the coexistence of Ag(0) and Ag(I) in GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs [25,26]. The peaks at 932.8 and 952.6 eV are assigned to the binding energies of Cu 2p3/2 and 2p1/2, respectively. Notably, there is no peak around 942.0 eV, suggesting the absence of Cu(II) in GS-CuNCs, GS-AgCuNCs, and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. S2B, Fig. 2E and 2F). Meanwhile, three peaks at 284.5, 285.5, and 287.9 eV appear in C 1s XPS spectra of GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. 2G and 2H), which are assigned to CH2CH3, CH- and COOH, respectively. Two peaks at 531.1 and 532.1 eV exist in O1s spectrum (Fig. 2I and 2J), which can be assigned to metal-O (CuO) and surface OH/CO groups, respectively [27].

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was applied to examine the size and surface morphology of NCs. Fig. 3 A demonstrates that GS-AgCuNCs are roughly spherical and monodispersive with an average size of 2.06 nm (Fig. S3). TEM image of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. 3B) indicates that a lot of aggregates of GS-AgCuNCs are assembled from dispersive nano-spheres. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) elemental mapping clearly suggests the presence of C, N, O, S, Cu, Ag, and Tb elements in Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. 3C). These findings demonstrate that GS-AgCuNCs aggregate in the presence of Tb3+ based on the strong affinity between Tb3+ and GS, thus inducing a significant AIE effect of GS-AgCuNCs. In addition, dynamic light scattering (DLS) characterization of GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. 3D and 3E) was carried out, demonstrating that the average size of the latter is significantly larger than that of the former. Such results are consistent with TEM characterization and further verify AIE performance of Tb3+-derived GS-AgCuNCs.

As shown in Fig. 4A, GS-AgCuNCs have stronger fluorescence intensity than GS-CuNCs when excited at 352 nm, which may be attributed to the doping of metal atoms [28]. Furthermore, it is obvious that the fluorescence intensity was further enhanced after introducing Tb3+ into GS-AgCuNCs. The maximum emission intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs was obtained at 564 nm with a large stokes shift of 212 nm when the excitation wavelength was set at 352 nm (Fig. 4A). As shown in Fig. S4A, the enhanced fluorescence intensity at 564 nm is derived from Tb3+-induced formation of aggregates of GS-AgCuNCs rather than free Tb3+ in solution. There are no notable shifts in emission wavelengths when the excitation wavelength changes from 332 to 422 nm (Fig. S4B). As depicted in Fig. 4B, a more significant absorption peak near 300 nm and a new shoulder peak at 370 nm appear in UV–vis absorption spectra of GS-AgCuNCs. After the formation of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs, the shape of the UV–vis absorption spectrum changes, and the absorbance intensity slightly increases. This phenomenon may be caused by the aggregation of GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. 4 B), which is consistent with previously reported literature [22].

To meet the requirement of practical use, it is very important to evaluate the stability of a fluorescence probe. Fig. 4C presents that the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs is not significantly depressed even at NaCl concentrations as high as 200 mM, indicating its excellent stability in high ionic strength solutions. As displayed in Fig. 4D, Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs also exhibit good photostability. pH stability results are demonstrated Fig. 4E, indicating that the fluorescence intensity reaches maximum at pH = 5.0, and then decreases drastically at higher pH. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that the surface charge of GS-AgCuNCs is related to pH of the solution. The surface charge of GS-AgCuNCs is positive at low pH and changes to negative with increasing pH. When pH is lower than 5.0, the electrostatic interaction between Tb3+ and GS-AgCuNCs is improved with increasing pH, leading to the formation of aggregates and enhanced fluorescence. Regrettably, the nucleation of GS-AgCuNCs may be destroyed at pH > 5.0, resulting in decreased fluorescence intensity [29,30]. Fig. 4F depicts that the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs changes slightly after half a month, while that of GS-AgCuNCs drops to 20% of the initial intensity after four days (Fig. S5), demonstrating that Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs has higher storage stability.

Subsequently, quantum yield (QY) and fluorescence lifetime of GS-AgCuNCs and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs were investigated. Interestingly, QY values are 0.25% and 1.98%, respectively, indicating that Tb3+-triggered aggregation of GS-AgCuNCs significantly increases QY by a factor of eight. Meanwhile, we measured the fluorescence lifetime of GS-AgCuNCs before and after the addition of Tb3+, and found that Tb3+-induced generation of aggregates exhibited longer fluorescence lifetime (Fig. S6 and Table S1). In summary, the enhanced fluorescence intensity, improved storage stability, and extended fluorescence lifetime are attributed to the AIE property of GS-AgCuNCs. The combination of Tb3+ and GS restricts the intramolecular vibration and rotation of the ligand, thereby reducing the nonradiative attenuation of the excited state and improving the fluorescence properties [22,31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of TG and MTX
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 portrays the detection mechanism of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs for TG and MTX. Fig. 5 A depicts the UV–vis absorption spectra of TG and MTX and excitation/emission spectra of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs, respectively. The absorption spectrum of TG overlaps with the excitation spectrum of the probe over a broad wavelength range of 287–370 nm. The fluorescence lifetime of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs almost remains unchanged in the absence and presence of TG, which evidences that the potential mechanism is static quenching rather than dynamic quenching (Fig. 5B). By comparing the UV–Vis spectra of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs, TG, and TG + Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs systems (Fig. S7), no new absorption peaks were found, indicating that no non-fluorescent ground static complex or chelate was formed [32,33]. As a common fluorescence quenching mechanism, IFE generally means that the excitation or emission spectrum of a fluorophore is absorbed by light-absorbing substances, thus reducing or even quenching the fluorescence intensity. However, unlike fluorescence resonance energy transfer, IFE hardly causes the change of fluorescence lifetime [34]. Based on the above results, it can be inferred that IFE mechanism is followed when sensing TG with Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. In addition, we investigated the potential mechanism of the quenching effect of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs by MTX. MTX has three absorption peaks at 243, 302, and 350 nm, which mostly overlap with the excitation spectrum of the probe (Fig. 5A). Furthermore, there is almost no change in the fluorescence lifetime before and after the introduction of MTX (Fig. 5B). As depicted in Fig. S7, there is also no non-fluorescence complex between the MTX and Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. These observations demonstrate that IFE mechanism is also followed for the sensing of MTX.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization for TG and MTX sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental parameters such as solution pH and reaction time are extremely significant for the sensing performance of a sensor, therefore, we investigated the effects of the parameters during the measurement of TG and MTX to maximize the fluorescence response of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. As displayed in Fig. S8A, F0-F (F 0 and F are the emission intensities of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs without and with TG) gradually increases with increasing pH until it reaches a maximum at pH = 5.0. Therefore, such a value was selected as the optimal pH value to detect TG with Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. Fig. S8B depicts that the optimal reaction time for the sensing of TG is 10 min. Similarly, for MTX assay, the optimum pH and reaction time are also 5.0 and 10 min, respectively (Fig. S8C, D), which were thus employed in subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs Based assay for TG and MTX sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent fluorescence properties of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs, their applications in anticancer drug detection were explored. Under the above-obtained optimized experimental conditions, Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs were employed for the fluorescence sensing of TG and MTX. Fig. S9 demonstrates the significant quenching effect of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs fluorescence by TG and MTX, which fully confirms the good sensing performance of the probe toward the targets. Fig. 6 A depicts that the emission intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs decreases gradually with increasing TG concentrations. Moreover, as displayed in Fig. 6B, F/F 0 (F and F 0 are the emission intensities of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs with and without TG) decreases linearly with TG concentrations in the range of 0–80 µM, and DL of TG was determined to be 0.080 µM (3S/k). Other assays for TG sensing are summarized in Table S2. Compared with reported studies, the present method exhibits the advantages of low DL and wide linear range for the quantification of TG. To study the selectivity of the sensing platform for TG, several possible interferences including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, CO3 2–, NO3 –, SO4 2-, L-arginine (L-Arg), L-alanine (L-Ala), L-leucine (L-Leu), L-proline (L-Pro), L-serine (L-Ser), glucose (Glu), D-galactose (D-Gal), ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), vitamin B1 (VB1), and valine (Val) were measured under the same conditions. Results show that the interferences have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs. (Fig. 6C and 6D). In addition, it should be mentioned that our method also avoids the complicated synthesis and purification steps of fluorescent probes, making it more applicable for practical applications.

Intriguingly, Fig. S8A indicates that F 0-F is negative at pH = 8.0, which indicates that the fluorescence intensity increases after adding TG. We tested the change of fluorescence intensity of the probe at pH = 8.0 in the presence of TG with different concentrations. The fluorescence intensity of the probe increases along with increasing TG concentrations, and there is a good linear relationship for F/F 0 versus TG concentration over the range of 2–40 μM (Fig. S10). This phenomenon is opposite to the response of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs to TG at pH = 5.0, which may be because that the addition of TG leads to the reformation of aggregates [35,36]. In addition, the changes in surface morphology of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs after adding PBS (pH = 8.0, 0.2 M) and TG were recorded by TEM (Fig. S11). It is evident that in the presence of PBS, the aggregates are almost completely dissociated and the monodispersity is good, which indicates that the strong interaction between PO4 3- and Tb3+ results in the dissociation of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs aggregates [37] (Fig. S11A). However, after the addition of TG, some small aggregates reform (Fig. S11B) and the fluorescence intensity recovers somewhat, which is in good agreement with the results of fluorescence spectra.

As displayed in Fig. 7 A and 7B, the emission intensity of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs decreases with increasing MTX concentrations, and the plots of relative fluorescence intensities exhibit good linear relationships in the range of 0–80 µM (F/F 0 = -0.0056[MTX] + 0.9642, R 2 = 0.9854) with DL value of 0.043 μM. Alternative sensing methods for MTX are summarized in Table S3, indicating that Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs possess low DL or broad response range for MTX. Subsequently, the specificity of this fluorescent probe for MTX sensing was studied. Some possible interfering substances including Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, CO3 2–, NO3 –, SO4 2-, L-Arg, L-Ala, L-Leu, L-Pro, L-Ser, Glu, D-Gal, AA, UA, VB1, and Val were tested. Fig. 7C and 7D depict that the fluorescence intensity changes significantly only in the presence of MTX, indicating that the detection method can be well applied to the detection of anticancer drugs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TG and MTX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The analysis of TG and MTX is a crucial task in clinical application, which can help to understand the adverse reactions caused by overdose in time and further improve the therapeutic effect. To evaluate the applicability of this sensing platform in real samples, it was applied to detect TG and MTX in human serum samples. TG and MTX with different concentrations were added into human serum samples followed by the analysis via Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs (Fig. S12). The recovery values of TG were calculated to be between 98.72% and 110.15% with relative standard deviation (RSD) values less than 4.27% (n = 3). MTX recoveries are in the range of 93.04%–101.78%, and RSD values are less than 4.70%. These results indicate that the sensing platform has outstanding analytical performance and remarkable application potential for TG and MTX detection in real samples.

In conclusion, a fluorescence sensing platform based on Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs was constructed for the determination of anticancer drugs. GS-AgCuNCs were synthesized employing a simple one-pot approach, subsequently, the introduction of Tb3+ triggered aggregation of GS-AgCuNCs leading to the formation Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs complexes. Compared with GS-AgCuNCs, Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs have stronger fluorescence intensity and stability, which suggests that AIE is an efficient method to improve the fluorescence properties and stability of BMNCs. In addition, the fluorescence of Tb3+@GS-AgCuNCs is quenched by TG and MTX via IFE, and the proposed method is simple, time-saving, and environment-friendly. This work not only provides a novel fluorescence probe for high sensitivity sensing of anticancer drugs, but also opens up an approach to explore the AIE effect of BMNCs and their applications in the sensing field.

Jin Mu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Huifeng Zhang: Supervision, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhenzhen Huang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Qiong Jia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Changchun Science and Technology Bureau, China (21ZGN35) and Jilin Province Development and Reform Commission, China (2022C010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122388.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hepatic sinusoidal obstruction syndrome and short-term application of 6-thioguanine in pediatric acute lymphoblastic leukemia
Leukemia

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Stanulla, E. Schaeffeler, A. Möricke, S. Buchmann, M. Zimmermann, S. Igel, K. Schmiegelow, C. Flotho, H. Hartmann, S. Illsinger, A. Sauerbrey, S.V. Junk, P. Schütte, L. Hinze, M. Lauten, S. Modlich, R. Kolb, C. Rossig, G. Schwabe, A.K. Gnekow, G. Fleischhack, P.G. Schlegel, H.J. Schünemann, C.P. Kratz, G. Cario, M. Schrappe, M. sb:title.sb:maintitle: SHMT inhibition is effective and synergizes with methotrexate in T-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia
Leukemia
